judgments.title: Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala
judgments.judges: Sikri C.J., Shelat, Grover, Hegde, Mukherjea, Reddy, Jaganmohan, Khanna, Mathew, Beg, Dwivedi, Chandrachud, Palekar JJ.
judgments.subject: Constitutional Law
judgments.summary: Landmark judgment establishing the Basic Structure doctrine — Parliament cannot amend the Constitution to destroy its basic features including supremacy of the Constitution, republican and democratic form of government, secularism, separation of powers, and judicial review.
judgments.full_text: The Court held by a 7:6 majority that while Parliament has wide powers to amend the Constitution under Article 368, it cannot use this power to destroy or abrogate the basic structure or framework of the Constitution...

judgments.title: Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India
judgments.judges: Bhagwati, Untwalia, Fazal Ali, Tulzapurkar, Desai, Singh, Iyer JJ.
judgments.subject: Fundamental Rights
judgments.summary: Expanded the scope of Article 21 — the right to life and personal liberty includes the right to travel abroad. Procedure established by law must be fair, just and reasonable, not merely enacted by Parliament.
judgments.full_text: The Court held that the expression 'procedure established by law' in Article 21 means procedure which is reasonable, fair and just. Article 21 is not to be read in isolation but in conjunction with Articles 14 and 19...

judgments.title: Indra Sawhney v. Union of India
judgments.judges: Venkatachaliah, Thommen, Ahmadi, Punchhi, Sawant, Kuldip Singh, Ramaswamy, Sahai, Pandian JJ.
judgments.subject: Reservation & Affirmative Action
judgments.summary: Upheld 27% OBC reservation in central government jobs but capped total reservations at 50%. Excluded creamy layer from OBC benefits. Held that reservation in promotions requires separate constitutional amendment.
judgments.full_text: The Court upheld the implementation of the Mandal Commission recommendations subject to the exclusion of the creamy layer and the 50% ceiling on reservations...

judgments.title: Vishaka v. State of Rajasthan
judgments.subject: Gender Justice & Workplace Harassment
judgments.summary: Laid down Vishaka Guidelines for prevention of sexual harassment at workplace until legislation is enacted. Held that sexual harassment violates Articles 14, 15 and 21. Led to the Sexual Harassment of Women at Workplace Act, 2013.
judgments.full_text: The Court laid down guidelines binding on all employers and institutions until suitable legislation is enacted. These guidelines were later codified in the POSH Act, 2013...

judgments.title: Shreya Singhal v. Union of India
judgments.subject: Free Speech & IT Law
judgments.summary: Struck down Section 66A of the Information Technology Act, 2000 as unconstitutional. Held that vague and overbroad restrictions on online speech violate Article 19(1)(a). Distinguished between discussion, advocacy and incitement.
judgments.full_text: The Court struck down Section 66A in its entirety, holding it to be violative of Article 19(1)(a) and not saved by Article 19(2)...

judgments.title: Navtej Singh Johar v. Union of India
judgments.judges: Misra C.J., Khanwilkar, Chandrachud, Nariman, Malhotra JJ.
judgments.subject: LGBTQ+ Rights
judgments.summary: Decriminalized consensual homosexual acts by reading down Section 377 IPC. Held that sexual orientation is integral to dignity and identity under Article 21. Overruled Suresh Kumar Koushal v. Naz Foundation.
judgments.full_text: The Court held that Section 377 IPC insofar as it criminalizes consensual sexual acts between adults in private is unconstitutional...

judgments.title: Justice K.S. Puttaswamy v. Union of India
judgments.judges: Khehar C.J., Bobde, Chandrachud, Nariman, Lalit, Goel, Sapre, Chelameswar, Nazeer JJ.
judgments.subject: Right to Privacy
judgments.summary: Recognized right to privacy as a fundamental right under Articles 14, 19 and 21. Overruled M.P. Sharma and Kharak Singh. Privacy includes informational privacy, decisional autonomy and bodily integrity.
judgments.full_text: The Court unanimously held that the right to privacy is protected as an intrinsic part of the right to life and personal liberty under Article 21...

judgments.title: M. Siddiq v. Mahant Suresh Das
judgments.judges: Gogoi C.J., Bobde, Chandrachud, Nazeer, Bhushan JJ.
judgments.subject: Property & Religious Disputes
judgments.summary: Ayodhya land dispute judgment. Held that the entire disputed land be handed over to a trust for construction of Ram temple. Directed allotment of 5 acres alternate land for mosque construction.
judgments.full_text: The Court held that the disputed land be handed over to a trust to be formed by the Central Government for construction of a Ram temple...

judgments.title: Court on its own motion v. Prashant Bhushan
judgments.case_number: Suo Motu Contempt Petition (Civil) No. 1 of 2020
judgments.subject: Contempt of Court
judgments.summary: Held advocate Prashant Bhushan guilty of criminal contempt for tweets scandalizing the judiciary. Imposed Re 1 symbolic fine. Discussed balance between free speech and contempt law.
judgments.full_text: The Court found the respondent guilty of criminal contempt for scandalizing the Court through tweets...

judgments.title: Vodafone International Holdings B.V. v. Union of India
judgments.judges: Kapadia C.J., Swatanter Kumar, Radhakrishnan JJ.
judgments.subject: Tax Law
judgments.summary: Held that offshore share transfer between two non-resident companies does not attract capital gains tax in India when the transfer does not have a territorial nexus with India. Landmark ruling on indirect transfers.
judgments.full_text: The Court held that the offshore transaction between two foreign companies did not have sufficient territorial nexus to attract Indian tax jurisdiction...

judgments.title: National Legal Services Authority v. Union of India
judgments.subject: Transgender Rights
judgments.summary: Recognized transgender persons as third gender. Directed government to treat them as socially and educationally backward class entitled to reservations. Upheld right to self-identify gender.
judgments.full_text: The Court recognized hijras, eunuchs and transgender persons as third gender and directed the government to extend reservations and welfare measures...

judgments.title: Laxmi v. Union of India
judgments.subject: Acid Attack & Criminal Law
judgments.summary: Directed states to regulate acid sale and provide compensation to acid attack victims. Led to amendments in IPC (Sections 326A, 326B) and Criminal Procedure Code for acid attack offences.
judgments.full_text: The Court issued comprehensive directions for regulation of acid sale, treatment and rehabilitation of victims...

judgments.title: M.C. Mehta v. Union of India (Oleum Gas Leak)
judgments.subject: Environmental Law
judgments.summary: Established absolute liability principle for hazardous industries — enterprises engaged in dangerous activities owe absolute duty to community. No exceptions available. Pioneering environmental jurisprudence in India.
judgments.full_text: The Court evolved the principle of absolute liability, going beyond the rule in Rylands v. Fletcher, holding that enterprises in hazardous industries owe absolute and non-delegable duty...

judgments.title: Common Cause v. Union of India (Euthanasia)
judgments.judges: Misra C.J., Khanwilkar, Chandrachud, Nariman, Malhotra JJ.
judgments.subject: Medical Ethics & Right to Die
judgments.summary: Recognized living will and passive euthanasia. Held that right to die with dignity is part of Article 21. Laid down detailed procedure for advance medical directives.
judgments.full_text: The Court held that the right to die with dignity is a fundamental right and recognized advance medical directives (living wills)...

judgments.title: Samatha v. State of Andhra Pradesh
judgments.subject: Tribal Rights & Fifth Schedule
judgments.summary: Held that transfer of tribal land to private parties for mining is void under Fifth Schedule. Government cannot alienate tribal land to non-tribals or private companies for mining activities.
judgments.full_text: The Court held that transfer of tribal land to non-tribals or private parties for mining is unconstitutional under the Fifth Schedule...

citations.context: Relied on right to privacy and dignity for decriminalization of Section 377
Expanded Maneka Gandhi's interpretation of Article 21
Privacy recognized as part of basic structure
Applied expanded Article 21 principles to online speech
Right to identity as part of personal liberty
Built upon NALSA recognition of gender identity
Right to die with dignity under Article 21
Sexual harassment violates right to life and liberty
Extended protective jurisprudence to acid attack victims
Equality as part of basic structure limits reservation policy
Right to wholesome environment under Article 21
Contempt law limits on free speech distinguished from Section 66A
Fifth Schedule protections as basic feature
Secularism as basic structure informed dispute resolution

proformas.title: Plaint under Order VII CPC
proformas.description: Standard format for filing a civil suit in Delhi courts
proformas.content:
IN THE COURT OF _____________ AT NEW DELHI

Civil Suit No. _____ of 20__

IN THE MATTER OF:
[Plaintiff Name], aged __ years, resident of _____________
                                                    ... Plaintiff

VERSUS

[Defendant Name], aged __ years, resident of _____________
                                                    ... Defendant

SUIT FOR RECOVERY OF RS. _______ /- ALONG WITH INTEREST AND COSTS

MOST RESPECTFULLY SHOWETH:

1. That the Plaintiff is a _____________ residing at the above address.

2. That the Defendant is _____________ residing at the above address.

3. That the cause of action arose at Delhi on _____________ when _____________.

4. That the suit is within limitation as the cause of action arose on _____________.

5. That the Plaintiff has not filed any other suit in respect of the same cause of action.

6. That the valuation of the suit for purposes of court fee and jurisdiction is Rs. _______.

PRAYER:
It is, therefore, most respectfully prayed that this Hon'ble Court may be pleased to:
(a) Pass a decree for Rs. _______ in favour of the Plaintiff;
(b) Award pendente lite and future interest @ __% per annum;
(c) Award costs of the suit;
(d) Pass such other order as this Hon'ble Court deems fit.

VERIFICATION:
I, _____________, the Plaintiff above-named, do hereby verify that the contents of paragraphs 1 to 6 are true to my personal knowledge and no part of it is false.

Verified at New Delhi on this ___ day of _______ 20__.

                                                    Plaintiff
Through Counsel

proformas.title: Regular Bail Application under Section 439 CrPC
proformas.description: Bail application format for Maharashtra courts
proformas.content:
IN THE COURT OF HON'BLE SESSIONS JUDGE AT _____________

Criminal Bail Application No. _____ of 20__

IN THE MATTER OF:
[Applicant Name], aged __ years, resident of _____________
                                                    ... Applicant/Accused

VERSUS

State of Maharashtra
                                                    ... Respondent

APPLICATION FOR REGULAR BAIL UNDER SECTION 439 CrPC

MOST RESPECTFULLY SHOWETH:

1. That the Applicant is falsely implicated in FIR No. _____ dated _______ registered at Police Station _____________ under Sections _____________ of IPC.

2. That the Applicant was arrested on _______ and is in judicial custody since then.

3. That the Applicant is a permanent resident of Maharashtra with deep roots in the community and is not a flight risk.

4. That the investigation is complete and the Applicant's custody is no longer required.

5. That the Applicant undertakes to abide by all conditions imposed by this Hon'ble Court.

PRAYER:
It is, therefore, prayed that the Applicant be released on bail on such terms and conditions as this Hon'ble Court deems fit.

                                                    Applicant
Through Counsel

proformas.title: Writ Petition under Article 226
proformas.description: Writ petition format for Karnataka High Court
proformas.content:
IN THE HIGH COURT OF KARNATAKA AT BENGALURU

Writ Petition No. _____ of 20__

IN THE MATTER OF:
[Petitioner Name]
                                                    ... Petitioner

VERSUS

[Respondent Authority]
                                                    ... Respondent

PETITION UNDER ARTICLE 226 OF THE CONSTITUTION OF INDIA

MOST RESPECTFULLY SHOWETH:

1. That the Petitioner is a citizen of India residing at _____________.

2. That the Respondent is a public authority exercising statutory powers.

3. That the impugned action/order dated _______ is arbitrary, illegal and violative of Articles 14, 19 and 21 of the Constitution.

4. That the Petitioner has no other equally efficacious remedy available.

5. That the cause of action arose on _______ when the impugned order was passed.

PRAYER:
(a) Issue a writ of mandamus directing the Respondent to _____________;
(b) Quash the impugned order dated _______;
(c) Award costs.

                                                    Petitioner
Through Counsel

proformas.title: Divorce Petition under Hindu Marriage Act
proformas.description: Mutual consent divorce petition for Tamil Nadu Family Courts
proformas.content:
IN THE FAMILY COURT AT _____________, TAMIL NADU

O.P. No. _____ of 20__

IN THE MATTER OF:
[Husband Name]                                    ... Petitioner/Husband
VERSUS
[Wife Name]                                       ... Respondent/Wife

PETITION FOR DIVORCE BY MUTUAL CONSENT UNDER SECTION 13B OF HMA

1. That the parties were married on _______ at _____________ as per Hindu rites.

2. That they have been living separately since _______ for a period exceeding one year.

3. That they have mutually agreed that the marriage should be dissolved.

4. That efforts for reconciliation have failed.

5. That the parties have settled all claims including maintenance and custody of children.

PRAYER:
Dissolve the marriage between the parties by a decree of divorce.

                                                    Petitioner
Through Counsel

proformas.title: Consumer Complaint under Section 35 CPA
proformas.description: Universal consumer complaint format applicable across all states
proformas.content:
BEFORE THE DISTRICT CONSUMER DISPUTES REDRESSAL COMMISSION
AT _____________

Consumer Complaint No. _____ of 20__

[Complainant Name], resident of _____________
                                                    ... Complainant

VERSUS

[Opposite Party/Company Name]
                                                    ... Opposite Party

COMPLAINT UNDER SECTION 35 OF CONSUMER PROTECTION ACT, 2019

1. That the Complainant is a consumer who purchased _____________ from the Opposite Party on _______ for Rs. _______.

2. That the Opposite Party committed deficiency in service / defect in goods as _____________.

3. That the Complainant made a written complaint to the Opposite Party on _______ but received no redressal.

4. That the Complainant has suffered mental agony and financial loss.

RELIEF SOUGHT:
(a) Refund of Rs. _______;
(b) Compensation of Rs. _______ for mental agony;
(c) Litigation costs of Rs. _______.

                                                    Complainant

proformas.title: Anticipatory Bail under Section 438 CrPC
proformas.description: Anticipatory bail application for UP courts
proformas.content:
IN THE COURT OF SESSIONS JUDGE AT _____________, U.P.

Anticipatory Bail Application No. _____ of 20__

[Applicant Name]                                  ... Applicant
VERSUS
State of Uttar Pradesh                            ... Respondent

APPLICATION UNDER SECTION 438 CrPC

1. That the Applicant apprehends arrest in connection with FIR No. _____ under Sections _____________ IPC.

2. That the Applicant is innocent and the FIR is motivated.

3. That the Applicant is willing to cooperate with investigation.

4. That no custodial interrogation is required.

PRAYER:
Grant anticipatory bail in the event of arrest.

                                                    Applicant
Through Counsel

proformas.title: Temporary Injunction under Order XXXIX Rule 1-2 CPC
proformas.description: Application for interim injunction in Gujarat courts
proformas.content:
IN THE COURT OF CIVIL JUDGE AT _____________, GUJARAT

Civil Suit No. _____ of 20__

Application under Order XXXIX Rules 1 & 2 CPC

[Plaintiff Name]                                  ... Applicant/Plaintiff
VERSUS
[Defendant Name]                                    ... Respondent/Defendant

APPLICATION FOR TEMPORARY INJUNCTION

1. That the Applicant has filed the accompanying suit for _____________.

2. That the Respondent is threatening to _____________ causing irreparable injury.

3. That the balance of convenience lies in favour of the Applicant.

4. That the Applicant has a prima facie case.

PRAYER:
Restrain the Respondent from _____________ during pendency of the suit.

                                                    Applicant
Through Counsel

proformas.title: Industrial Dispute Reference Application
proformas.description: Application for reference of industrial dispute in West Bengal
proformas.content:
BEFORE THE LABOUR COURT AT _____________, WEST BENGAL

I.D. Case No. _____ of 20__

[Workman Name]                                    ... Claimant
VERSUS
[Management/Company Name]                         ... Management

STATEMENT OF CLAIM UNDER INDUSTRIAL DISPUTES ACT, 1947

1. That the Claimant was employed as _____________ since _______.

2. That the Management terminated the services on _______ without following principles of natural justice.

3. That the termination is illegal, unjustified and malafide.

4. That the Claimant is entitled to reinstatement with full back wages.

PRAYER:
(a) Reinstatement with continuity of service;
(b) Full back wages from date of termination;
(c) All consequential benefits.

                                                    Claimant
Through Union/Counsel

filing_outlines.title: Filing a Civil Suit in Delhi
filing_outlines.limitation_period: Varies by cause of action — typically 3 years for money recovery (Article 85, Limitation Act)
filing_outlines.court_fee: As per Court Fees Act, 1870 — ad valorem on suit value. Delhi Court Fees (Delhi Amendment) Act applies.

steps.title: Determine Jurisdiction
steps.detail: Ascertain territorial jurisdiction (where cause of action arose or defendant resides) and pecuniary jurisdiction (District Court up to Rs. 2 crore, High Court above).

steps.title: Calculate Court Fee
steps.detail: Compute suit valuation and purchase court fee stamps from authorized vendors or pay through e-court fee system.

steps.title: Draft Plaint
steps.detail: Prepare plaint per Order VII CPC with cause of action, jurisdiction, limitation, and prayer clauses.

steps.title: E-Filing on Delhi High Court Portal
steps.detail: Register on https://delhihighcourt.nic.in e-filing portal. Upload plaint, vakalatnama, and annexures in PDF format.

steps.title: Physical Filing (District Courts)
steps.detail: File at the filing counter of the concerned District Court with original and copies. Obtain diary number.

steps.title: Defect Rectification
steps.detail: If objections are raised, rectify within the given time (usually 7 days) and resubmit.

steps.title: Summons & Service
steps.detail: Upon registration, court issues summons. Arrange service through court process server or registered post.

steps.title: First Hearing
steps.detail: Appear on the date fixed. Court may refer to mediation (Delhi Mediation Rules) or frame issues.

tips: Delhi courts mandate e-filing for High Court matters
Check cause list daily on court website
Mediation is encouraged at pre-institution stage for commercial disputes

filing_outlines.title: Filing a Criminal Complaint in Maharashtra
filing_outlines.limitation_period: No limitation for cognizable offences. For private complaints, generally no limitation except as specified.
filing_outlines.court_fee: Nominal court fee for complaint. No ad valorem fee for criminal matters.

steps.title: Choose Forum
steps.detail: Magistrate Court for offences punishable up to 3 years. Sessions Court for graver offences. Can file complaint under Section 200 CrPC.

steps.title: Draft Complaint
steps.detail: Set out facts constituting the offence, names of accused, and relief sought (summoning of accused, trial).

steps.title: File at Filing Counter
steps.detail: Submit at the concerned Metropolitan Magistrate or JMFC court. Mumbai has dedicated filing centers at City Civil Court.

steps.title: Swearing of Complainant
steps.detail: Complainant must swear to the complaint on oath before the Magistrate under Section 200 CrPC.

steps.title: Magisterial Inquiry
steps.detail: Magistrate may conduct inquiry or investigation under Section 202 CrPC before issuing process.

steps.title: Summons to Accused
steps.detail: If prima facie case exists, Magistrate issues summons or warrant under Section 204 CrPC.

steps.title: Trial Commencement
steps.detail: Charge is framed. Prosecution leads evidence. Defence cross-examines. Arguments and judgment follow.

tips: For cheque bounce cases, follow Section 138 NI Act special procedure
Maharashtra has fast-track courts for specific offences
Online case status available on MahaCourts portal

filing_outlines.title: Filing a Writ Petition in Karnataka High Court
filing_outlines.limitation_period: No fixed limitation for writ petitions, but delay must be explained. Laches doctrine applies.
filing_outlines.court_fee: Rs. 50 for writ petition as per Karnataka Court Fees Act. Additional fee for interim applications.
documents_required: Writ petition with synopsis and list of dates

steps.title: Exhaust Alternative Remedies
steps.detail: Writ is extraordinary remedy. Show that no equally efficacious remedy exists or why interim relief cannot wait.

steps.title: Draft Writ Petition
steps.detail: Include facts, grounds (violation of Articles 14/19/21), prayer for appropriate writ (mandamus, certiorari, prohibition, habeas corpus).

steps.title: E-Filing on HC Karnataka Portal
steps.detail: File through https://karnatakajudiciary.kar.nic.in e-filing system with digital signatures.

steps.title: Listing & Admission
steps.detail: Matter is listed before roster bench. Court hears admission. Notice may be issued to respondents.

steps.title: Interim Relief
steps.detail: If urgent, file interim application (IA) along with writ. Court may grant stay of impugned order.

steps.title: Counter Affidavit
steps.detail: Respondent files counter. Rejoinder may be filed by petitioner.

steps.title: Final Hearing & Judgment
steps.detail: Arguments on merits. Court passes final order allowing or dismissing the writ.

tips: Bengaluru bench handles most writs; Dharwad and Kalaburagi benches have regional jurisdiction
Mentioning before Registrar for urgent listing
Public Interest Litigations have separate cell

filing_outlines.title: Filing for Divorce in Tamil Nadu
filing_outlines.limitation_period: No limitation for divorce petition. Mutual consent requires 1 year separation (Section 13B HMA).
filing_outlines.court_fee: As per Tamil Nadu Court Fees Act — typically Rs. 15-50 for family court petitions.

steps.title: Determine Applicable Law
steps.detail: HMA for Hindu marriages, SMA for inter-faith, Dissolution of Muslim Marriages Act for Muslims, Indian Divorce Act for Christians.

steps.title: Choose Grounds
steps.detail: Fault-based (Section 13): cruelty, adultery, desertion. Mutual consent (Section 13B): living separately for 1+ year.

steps.title: File at Family Court
steps.detail: Family Courts in Chennai, Coimbatore, Madurai, Trichy, etc. File petition with required documents.

steps.title: First Motion (Mutual Consent)
steps.detail: Both parties appear. Court records statements. 6-month cooling period begins (reducible to 6 weeks in certain cases per 2021 amendment).

steps.title: Second Motion
steps.detail: After cooling period, parties reaffirm consent. Court grants divorce decree.

steps.title: Contested Divorce
steps.detail: If contested, court refers to mediation. Evidence recorded. Decree granted if grounds proved.

tips: Family Courts are mandatory for matrimonial disputes in Tamil Nadu
Mediation is compulsory before trial in contested cases
Maintenance under Section 125 CrPC can be filed simultaneously

filing_outlines.title: Filing a Consumer Complaint in Kerala
filing_outlines.limitation_period: 2 years from the date of cause of action (Section 69, CPA 2019)
filing_outlines.court_fee: No court fee for consumer complaints. Nominal filing charges may apply.

steps.title: Identify Correct Forum
steps.detail: District Commission: claims up to Rs. 50 lakh. State Commission: Rs. 50 lakh to Rs. 2 crore. National Commission: above Rs. 2 crore.

steps.title: Send Legal Notice
steps.detail: Though not mandatory, send notice to opposite party giving 15-30 days to resolve. Strengthens case.

steps.title: Draft Complaint
steps.detail: Set out facts, deficiency in service or defect in goods, and relief sought (refund, replacement, compensation).

steps.title: File Online or Physically
steps.detail: E-daakhil portal (https://edaakhil.nic.in) for online filing. Or file at District Consumer Commission office.

steps.title: Notice to Opposite Party
steps.detail: Commission issues notice. Opposite party files version/written reply.

steps.title: Evidence & Hearing
steps.detail: Affidavit evidence. Cross-examination if required. Commission passes order.

steps.title: Execution
steps.detail: If order not complied, file execution petition before the same Commission.

tips: Kerala has active consumer forums in all 14 districts
E-daakhil enables paperless filing nationwide
No advocate required — consumers can appear in person

filing_outlines.title: Applying for Bail in Uttar Pradesh
filing_outlines.limitation_period: No limitation — bail can be sought at any stage of proceedings.
filing_outlines.court_fee: No court fee for bail applications.

steps.title: Determine Bail Type
steps.detail: Regular bail (Section 439) after arrest. Anticipatory bail (Section 438) before arrest. Default bail (Section 167(2)) if investigation not completed in 60/90 days.

steps.title: Draft Application
steps.detail: Include FIR details, role of accused, grounds for bail (no flight risk, no tampering, medical grounds, etc.).

steps.title: File at Sessions Court
steps.detail: For most offences, Sessions Court is first forum for regular bail. For AB, Sessions or High Court.

steps.title: Notice to Prosecution
steps.detail: Court issues notice to AGA (Assistant Government Advocate). Prosecution files reply.

steps.title: Hearing
steps.detail: Arguments on merits. Court considers nature of offence, evidence, and antecedents.

steps.title: Bail Order
steps.detail: If granted, execute bail bonds and surety. Accused is released from custody.

steps.title: High Court if Rejected
steps.detail: Approach Allahabad High Court (Lucknow or Prayagraj bench) under Section 439.

tips: UP has specific restrictions on anticipatory bail in certain offences
Surety must be local resident with verifiable property
Check eCourts portal for case status

filing_outlines.title: Filing an Industrial Dispute in West Bengal
filing_outlines.court: Labour Court / Industrial Tribunal / Labour Appellate Tribunal
filing_outlines.limitation_period: 3 years for reference of industrial dispute (Limitation Act applies)
filing_outlines.court_fee: No court fee for industrial dispute applications.

steps.title: Raise Industrial Dispute
steps.detail: Workman or trade union raises dispute with management. Mandatory for organized sector.

steps.title: Conciliation
steps.detail: Conciliation Officer (under ID Act) attempts settlement within 14 days.

steps.title: Government Reference
steps.detail: If conciliation fails, State Government refers dispute to Labour Court/Industrial Tribunal.

steps.title: Filing Statement of Claim
steps.detail: Workman files claim before the Tribunal with all supporting documents.

steps.title: Management's Written Statement
steps.detail: Management files reply denying allegations or justifying termination.

steps.title: Evidence & Arguments
steps.detail: Both parties lead evidence. Tribunal examines witnesses.

steps.title: Award
steps.detail: Tribunal passes award. Becomes operative after 30 days unless Government modifies.

tips: West Bengal has separate Labour Appellate Tribunal
Single workman disputes can go directly to Labour Court in certain cases
Retrenchment compensation under Section 25F is a common claim

filing_outlines.title: Filing a Public Interest Litigation in Supreme Court
filing_outlines.limitation_period: No fixed limitation, but prompt filing expected. Delay must be explained.
filing_outlines.court_fee: Rs. 50 for writ petition in Supreme Court.

steps.title: Establish Public Interest
steps.detail: PIL must raise a public law issue affecting a class of people, not a private grievance. Locus standi is relaxed.

steps.title: Draft Petition
steps.detail: Follow Supreme Court writ proforma. Include precise prayers for directions to State/authority.

steps.title: Filing at Registry
steps.detail: File at Supreme Court filing counter or through e-filing. Pay court fee. Obtain diary number.

steps.title: Registry Scrutiny
steps.detail: Registry examines petition for defects. Rectify objections within stipulated time.

steps.title: Listing Before Court
steps.detail: Matter listed before appropriate bench. Court may issue notice or dismiss at admission.

steps.title: Interim Directions
steps.detail: Court may pass interim orders for immediate relief (e.g., stop illegal construction, provide medical aid).

steps.title: Final Order
steps.detail: After affidavits and arguments, Court passes binding directions. Non-compliance amounts to contempt.

tips: PIL must be bonafide — courts penalize publicity-seeking petitions
Amicus curiae may be appointed for complex matters
Supreme Court Legal Services Committee can assist indigent petitioners

cases.acts_sections: Commercial Courts Act, 2015 — Recovery of dues
cases.summary: Commercial suit for recovery of Rs. 45 lakhs arising from breach of supply contract.

orders.order_details: Notice issued to respondent. Reply filed.
Issues framed. Matter listed for evidence.

cases.summary: Petition under Section 482 CrPC seeking quashing of FIR No. 145/2020 registered at Koramangala Police Station.

orders.order_details: Notice issued to State. Stay of investigation granted.

cases.judges: Shri V.D. Patil, Civil Judge (Senior Division)
cases.summary: Mutual consent divorce petition. Decree granted on 10-12-2024.

orders.order_details: Decree of divorce granted by mutual consent.
